files.SKILL.md:
# Glossary for boxed terminals

**Verifier** — any command the instructions treat as authoritative for pass or fail.

**Surface** — the filesystem and processes you can reach from the current shell; if
listings disagree with the brief, your surface is wrong before the code is wrong.

**Burst** — a set of tool calls that can all run without waiting on unknown paths;
batch them together.

**Lane** — a single file or coherent directory tree you edit without overlapping
unrelated writes on the same path.

**Smoke** — the smallest command that exercises the last change (compile one file,
run one test node, print one config). For any write that produces a named output
file, the smoke includes: confirm the file exists at the exact required path with
non-zero size; for structured output (CSV, JSON, delimited text), read back header,
first row, last row, and total row count before running the verifier.

**Fence** — boundaries the brief draws; outside the fence is not your job this
episode.

**Handoff** — one durable sentence future you can trust; not a transcript.

**Mechanism** — the exact pattern, parser, filter, or logic already present in
existing code; must be confirmed by reading the source, not inferred. When a task
requires modifying or working around existing code, the mechanism is unknown until
cited from the file.

**Gate** — a concurrency check evaluated at the moment a work unit attempts to
start; not at schedule time. Units beyond the cap queue behind the gate. A queued
unit that has not yet passed the gate must never start once shutdown is in progress.

**Lifecycle** — the full execution path of a concurrent work unit: start, run,
cancel, and cleanup. Cleanup must execute whether the unit completes normally or is
cancelled, and must produce observable output. Signal handlers that trigger
lifecycle shutdown must be registered before any unit starts.

---

## Application

Start by making the verifier line concrete — including the exact output path the
task requires. Freeze the fence. When the task touches existing code, confirm the
mechanism before designing the patch. Run bursts for discovery, then one lane at a
time with a smoke between lanes. When the verifier passes with positive evidence —
not merely absence of errors — write a handoff only if you learned a pattern, not a
diary entry.

If a command fails twice with the same shape, change the diagnosis — not the
decoration on the same command.

---

## Concurrency contract

When the task involves concurrent work execution, the implementation must satisfy:

- Async-native primitives over thread pools — thread pools do not propagate
  cancellation; async primitives do.
- A **gate** per unit at start time — not a single gather over all coroutines.
  Scheduling all coroutines at once bypasses the gate.
- **Lifecycle** discipline for every unit — cleanup path runs on cancel and on
  normal exit; output is observable in both cases.
- Signal handlers registered before any work starts — on signal: stop intake,
  cancel in-flight units, await cleanup, then exit.
- Exit codes reflect actual outcome; exceptions surface to stderr, never suppressed.

---

## Data contract

When the task produces a structured output file:

- Exact path as named in the brief — no variation in filename, extension, or
  directory.
- Format matched character-for-character: header names, row order, delimiters,
  quoting, line endings.
- Categorical fields parsed with exact-match or word-boundary logic — substring
  matching over-counts.
- Mechanism confirmed from a sample of actual input before writing parsing logic —
  never assume field names or structure.
- Date and time range boundaries applied exactly as stated; inclusive means
  inclusive on both ends unless told otherwise; date references derived from the
  task, not system time.
- Smoke applied after writing: read back header, first row, last row, row count,
  then run the verifier.
